---
title: "Benchmarking TorchTextClassifiers at INSEE"
author: "Julien Bernardi"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: false
lang: en
execute:
  python: .venv/bin/python
  echo: false
keep-ipynb: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
  figcaption, .figure-caption { margin-top: 14px; }
</style>
```

## Introduction

This report presents the work carried out during an internship at **INSEE** (Institut national de la statistique et des études économiques), France's national statistics institute.

The internship focuses on **text classification**, a core natural language processing (NLP) task that consists in assigning a predefined category to a piece of text. At INSEE, a concrete use case is the automatic coding of business creation declarations: when a company is founded, its activity is described in free text by the declarant and must be assigned a code from the NAF (*Nomenclature des Activités Françaises*), a hierarchical classification of economic activities. A model has recently been deployed to automate this coding process, making it a concrete reference point for evaluating the lightweight architectures studied in this internship.

Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on text classification, but they come with significant computational costs. In a production environment processing millions of records, lighter and faster alternatives are desirable. This internship investigates whether **lightweight neural architectures** — such as FastText-style mean-pooling models and small transformer encoders — can reach competitive accuracy at a fraction of the cost. All experiments are built on top of [**`torchTextClassifiers`**](https://github.com/InseeFrLab/torchTextClassifiers), a Python package developed at INSEE that provides a unified interface for training and evaluating these architectures.

The main objectives are:

1. Benchmark lightweight text classification models across several datasets.
2. Study the impact of key hyperparameters: embedding dimension, number of transformer layers, number of attention heads, vocabulary size, and learning rate.
3. Evaluate the trade-off between model size (number of parameters) and classification accuracy, through systematic validation loss curves.
4. Compare three architecture families — from simple mean-pooling to attention-based models — to understand when the added complexity pays off.

## Benchmark Setup

### Datasets

The benchmark covers two publicly available sentiment analysis datasets of increasing difficulty.

**IMDb** (`stanfordnlp/imdb`) is the Large Movie Review Dataset [@maas2011]. It contains 25,000 training and 25,000 test English movie reviews, each labelled as positive or negative (reviews with a score ≥ 7/10 or ≤ 4/10 on IMDb). The binary nature of the task and the length of the reviews make it a standard NLP benchmark. SOTA accuracy reaches ~97% with large transformer ensembles; RoBERTa alone achieves ~95%.

**Amazon Reviews** (`amazon_reviews_multi`, English) is the Multilingual Amazon Reviews Corpus (MARC) introduced by @keung2020. The English split contains 200,000 training and 5,000 test product reviews, each rated from 1 to 5 stars. The input combines the review title and body. The 5-class ordinal nature of the task makes it harder than binary sentiment: the mBERT baseline from the original paper reaches 63.3% accuracy.

In our experiments, 2,000 (IMDb) and 5,000 (Amazon) training examples are held out as a validation set, leaving 23,000 and 195,000 examples for training respectively.

| Dataset | Classes | Train | Val | Test | SOTA (acc.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| IMDb | 2 | 23,000 | 2,000 | 5,000 | ~97% (ensemble) |
| Amazon reviews | 5 | 195,000 | 5,000 | 5,000 | 63.3% (mBERT) |

### Models

Three architectures of increasing complexity are compared:

- **FastText-style** (`n_layers=0`): embeddings are averaged across tokens and fed directly to a classification head. No attention mechanism, minimal parameter count, very fast at both training and inference.
- **Transformer encoder** (`n_layers=1,2`): a small transformer encoder block (multi-head self-attention + feed-forward) is stacked on top of the embeddings before the classification head. Captures contextual interactions between tokens.
- **Label Attention**: an attention mechanism where the query is derived from label representations, allowing the model to focus on the parts of the input that are most relevant to each candidate class. This hybrid architecture sits between the transformer encoder and a full cross-attention model.

All three architectures use a **WordPiece tokenizer** trained on the training data, with vocabulary sizes ranging from 2,000 to 20,000 tokens.

### Experimental Setup

All experiments are tracked with **MLflow** and run on GPU via **Argo Workflows** on the INSEE datalab infrastructure. Each configuration is trained for up to 40 epochs with early stopping (patience = 5).

The grid search covers 624 configurations in total. For FastText (`n_layers=0`), `n_head` and label attention are not applicable, yielding $4 \times 4 \times 3 = 48$ runs. For transformer architectures (`n_layers` ∈ {1, 2}), the full grid gives $4 \times 2 \times 3 \times 2 \times 4 \times 3 = 576$ runs.

| Hyperparameter | Values | Applies to |
|---|---|---|
| Embedding dimension | 64, 128, 256, 512 | All |
| Number of layers (`n_layers`) | 0, 1, 2 | All |
| Vocabulary size | 2,000 / 5,000 / 10,000 / 20,000 | All |
| Learning rate | 0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001 | All |
| Attention heads (`n_head`) | 2, 4, 8 | `n_layers` ≥ 1 |
| Label attention (`n_heads_label_attention`) | none, 4 | `n_layers` ≥ 1 |

## Experimental Study

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Amazon

```{python}
#| label: fetch-mlflow-amazon

from pathlib import Path
import mlflow
import pandas as pd

CACHE_PATH = Path("benchmark/results/amazon_runs.csv")
CACHE_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

if CACHE_PATH.exists():
    df = pd.read_csv(CACHE_PATH)
    if "metrics.train_time_s" in df.columns:
        df = df.rename(columns={"metrics.train_time_s": "train_time_s"})
else:
    df = mlflow.search_runs(experiment_names=["amazon"], max_results=2000)
    df = df[df["status"] == "FINISHED"].copy()
    df = df.rename(columns={
        "params.emb_dim":                  "emb_dim",
        "params.n_layers":                 "n_layers",
        "params.n_head":                   "n_head",
        "params.n_heads_label_attention":  "n_heads_label_attn",
        "params.vocab_size":               "vocab_size",
        "params.lr":                       "lr",
        "metrics.num_params":              "num_params",
        "metrics.test_accuracy":           "test_accuracy",
        "metrics.test_f1_macro":           "test_f1_macro",
        "metrics.train_time_s":            "train_time_s",
    })

    client = mlflow.MlflowClient()
    def get_min_val_loss(run_id):
        history = client.get_metric_history(run_id, "val_loss")
        return min(m.value for m in history) if history else None

    df["min_val_loss"] = df["run_id"].apply(get_min_val_loss)

    for col in ["emb_dim", "n_layers", "vocab_size"]:
        df[col] = df[col].astype(int)
    df["lr"] = df["lr"].astype(float)
    df["num_params"] = df["num_params"].astype(int)

    def arch_label(row):
        if row["n_layers"] == 0:
            return "FastText"
        elif str(row["n_heads_label_attn"]) == "4":
            return "Label Attention"
        else:
            return f"Transformer (L={row['n_layers']})"

    df["architecture"] = df.apply(arch_label, axis=1)
    df.to_csv(CACHE_PATH, index=False)
```

### Validation loss vs. number of parameters & stability

A key observation is that **`n_head` and `lr` do not affect `num_params`**. In a standard transformer, the total size of the attention matrices is $4 \times d^2$ regardless of how many heads are used, since $d_{\text{head}} = d / n_{\text{head}}$. Similarly, `lr` is a training hyperparameter with no effect on architecture. As a result, for any fixed combination of `(emb_dim, n_layers, vocab_size)`, there are exactly $3 \times 3 = 9$ runs sharing the same `num_params` (3 values of `n_head` × 3 values of `lr`).

This motivates two complementary views on the first tab:

- **Scatter plot**: all 624 runs, showing the full spread of achievable val_loss for a given parameter budget.
- **Curve plot**: for each `(architecture, emb_dim, vocab_size)` group, take the `min(val_loss)` over `n_head` and `lr`, collapsing the 9 runs into a single best-case point. Sorting by `num_params` then yields one clean curve per architecture, tracing the performance frontier as model capacity grows.

The second tab shows the **distribution of validation loss across all runs** for each architecture. A narrow violin indicates that the architecture is robust to hyperparameter choice, while a wide one signals high sensitivity.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Val loss vs. parameters

```{python}
#| label: fig-pareto
#| fig-cap: "Validation loss vs. number of parameters"

import plotly.graph_objects as go

COLORS = {
    "FastText":           "#2196F3",
    "Transformer (L=1)": "#FF9800",
    "Transformer (L=2)": "#F44336",
    "Label Attention":   "#4CAF50",
}

df_curves = (
    df.groupby(["architecture", "emb_dim", "vocab_size", "num_params"], as_index=False)
    ["min_val_loss"].min()
    .sort_values("num_params")
)

df_ft      = df[df["architecture"] == "FastText"].groupby(
    ["architecture", "emb_dim", "vocab_size", "lr", "num_params"], as_index=False
)["min_val_loss"].min()
df_scatter = pd.concat([df_ft, df[df["architecture"] != "FastText"]], ignore_index=True)

fig = go.Figure()

for arch, group in df_scatter.groupby("architecture"):
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["num_params"], y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="markers",
        name=arch, legendgroup=arch,
        marker=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), size=5, opacity=0.3),
        hovertemplate="params=%{x:,}<br>val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
    ))

for arch, group in df_curves.groupby("architecture"):
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["num_params"], y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="lines+markers",
        name=arch, legendgroup=arch, showlegend=False,
        line=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), width=2),
        marker=dict(size=5),
        hovertemplate="params=%{x:,}<br>best val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Number of parameters",
    yaxis_title="Validation loss",
    legend_title="Architecture",
    height=450,
    xaxis=dict(tickformat=".2s"),
)
fig.show()
```

### Stability

```{python}
#| label: fig-violin
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of validation loss by architecture"

arch_order = ["FastText", "Transformer (L=1)", "Transformer (L=2)", "Label Attention"]
arch_order = [a for a in arch_order if a in df["architecture"].unique()]

fig = go.Figure()
for arch in arch_order:
    color = COLORS.get(arch, "grey")
    vals  = df[df["architecture"] == arch]["min_val_loss"].dropna()
    # Violin
    fig.add_trace(go.Violin(
        y=vals, name=arch,
        side="both",
        fillcolor=color, opacity=0.25,
        line=dict(color=color, width=1.5),
        box_visible=False, meanline_visible=False,
        points=False,
        showlegend=False,
        hoverinfo="skip",
    ))
    # Box slim on top
    fig.add_trace(go.Box(
        y=vals, name=arch,
        marker=dict(color=color, size=3, opacity=0.4),
        line=dict(color=color, width=1.5),
        fillcolor="white",
        boxmean="sd",
        width=0.12,
        showlegend=False,
        hovertemplate="<b>" + arch + "</b><br>median=%{median:.4f}<br>Q1=%{q1:.4f}<br>Q3=%{q3:.4f}<extra></extra>",
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    template="simple_white",
    yaxis_title="Validation loss",
    yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e8e8e8"),
    showlegend=False,
    height=430,
    violingap=0.4,
    boxgap=0.4,
)
fig.show()
```

:::

**Val loss vs. parameters.** FastText plateaus around 0.94 regardless of model size, showing that averaging embeddings without any attention mechanism hits a hard performance ceiling. The two transformer variants improve steadily with capacity: Transformer (L=2) reaches the lowest validation loss (~0.87) and remains the best architecture across all parameter budgets. Transformer (L=1) sits in between, around 0.89–0.90. Label Attention is noisier — its curve oscillates more than the others, suggesting higher sensitivity to the specific hyperparameter combination.

**Stability.** FastText is by far the most stable architecture: its violin is narrow and concentrated around 0.94–0.95, meaning the choice of hyperparameters has little impact on the outcome. Transformer (L=2) achieves the best median performance but at the cost of a wide distribution (~0.86–0.91), indicating that some configurations work much better than others. Label Attention shows a similar spread to Transformer (L=2) but with a slightly higher median, making it both sensitive and less performant on average.

### Effect of vocabulary size and embedding dimension

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Vocabulary size

```{python}
#| label: fig-vocab
#| fig-cap: "Effect of vocabulary size on validation loss"

import numpy as np

vocab_vals = sorted(df["vocab_size"].unique())
emb_vals   = sorted(df["emb_dim"].unique())

df_vocab_mean = (
    df.groupby(["architecture", "vocab_size"], as_index=False)["min_val_loss"].mean()
    .sort_values("vocab_size")
)
df_emb_mean = (
    df.groupby(["architecture", "emb_dim"], as_index=False)["min_val_loss"].mean()
    .sort_values("emb_dim")
)

vocab_offsets = {
    "FastText":           0,
    "Transformer (L=1)": -250,
    "Transformer (L=2)": +250,
    "Label Attention":   0,
}

fig = go.Figure()
for arch, group in df.groupby("architecture"):
    offset = vocab_offsets[arch]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["vocab_size"] + offset, y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="markers", name=arch, legendgroup=arch,
        marker=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), size=5, opacity=0.3),
        hovertemplate="vocab=%{customdata:,}<br>val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
        customdata=group["vocab_size"],
    ))
for arch, group in df_vocab_mean.groupby("architecture"):
    offset = vocab_offsets.get(arch, 0)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["vocab_size"] + offset, y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="lines+markers", name=arch, legendgroup=arch, showlegend=False,
        line=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), width=2), marker=dict(size=6),
        hovertemplate="vocab=%{customdata:,}<br>mean val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
        customdata=group["vocab_size"],
    ))
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Vocabulary size", yaxis_title="Validation loss",
    xaxis=dict(tickvals=vocab_vals, ticktext=[f"{v:,}" for v in vocab_vals]),
    legend_title="Architecture", height=430, width=750, margin=dict(l=60, r=20),
)
fig.show()
```

### Embedding dimension

```{python}
#| label: fig-emb
#| fig-cap: "Effect of embedding dimension on validation loss"

emb_offsets = {
    "FastText":           0,
    "Transformer (L=1)": -6,
    "Transformer (L=2)": +6,
    "Label Attention":   0,
}

fig = go.Figure()
for arch, group in df.groupby("architecture"):
    offset = emb_offsets[arch]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["emb_dim"] + offset, y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="markers", name=arch, legendgroup=arch,
        marker=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), size=5, opacity=0.3),
        hovertemplate="emb_dim=%{customdata}<br>val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
        customdata=group["emb_dim"],
    ))
for arch, group in df_emb_mean.groupby("architecture"):
    offset = emb_offsets.get(arch, 0)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["emb_dim"] + offset, y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="lines+markers", name=arch, legendgroup=arch, showlegend=False,
        line=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), width=2), marker=dict(size=6),
        hovertemplate="emb_dim=%{customdata}<br>mean val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
        customdata=group["emb_dim"],
    ))
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Embedding dimension", yaxis_title="Validation loss",
    xaxis=dict(tickvals=emb_vals, ticktext=[str(v) for v in emb_vals]),
    legend_title="Architecture", height=430, width=750, margin=dict(l=60, r=20),
)
fig.show()
```

:::

**Vocabulary size.** All architectures benefit modestly from a larger vocabulary, but with diminishing returns after 10,000 tokens. FastText remains largely unaffected, barely improving from 0.952 at 2k to 0.940 at 20k. Transformer-based models show a clearer gain up to 10k, after which the curves flatten — suggesting that most of the useful vocabulary is captured within that range for this dataset.

**Embedding dimension.** The picture is similar: performance improves as embedding size grows from 64 to 256, but gains stall or even reverse at 512. Transformer (L=2) peaks at 256 (~0.873) then slightly degrades, suggesting that very large embeddings bring no benefit and may introduce optimization difficulties. FastText is again nearly insensitive to this parameter. For transformer architectures, **256 appears to be the sweet spot**.

### Effect of number of attention heads

```{python}
#| label: fig-nhead
#| fig-cap: "Effect of number of attention heads on validation loss"

df_attn = df[df["n_layers"] > 0].copy()
df_attn["n_head"] = df_attn["n_head"].astype(int)
head_vals = sorted(df_attn["n_head"].unique())

df_head_min = (
    df_attn.groupby(["architecture", "n_head"], as_index=False)["min_val_loss"].mean()
    .sort_values("n_head")
)

head_offsets = {
    "Transformer (L=1)": -0.075,
    "Transformer (L=2)": +0.075,
    "Label Attention":   0,
}

fig = go.Figure()
for arch, group in df_attn.groupby("architecture"):
    offset = head_offsets.get(arch, 0)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["n_head"] + offset, y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="markers", name=arch, legendgroup=arch,
        marker=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), size=5, opacity=0.3),
        hovertemplate="n_head=%{customdata}<br>val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
        customdata=group["n_head"],
    ))
for arch, group in df_head_min.groupby("architecture"):
    offset = head_offsets.get(arch, 0)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["n_head"] + offset, y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="lines+markers", name=arch, legendgroup=arch, showlegend=False,
        line=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), width=2), marker=dict(size=6),
        hovertemplate="n_head=%{customdata}<br>mean val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
        customdata=group["n_head"],
    ))
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Number of attention heads", yaxis_title="Validation loss",
    xaxis=dict(tickvals=head_vals, ticktext=[str(v) for v in head_vals]),
    legend_title="Architecture", height=420,
)
fig.show()
```

The number of attention heads has virtually no effect on validation loss across all three architectures. The mean curves are essentially flat from 2 to 8 heads, and the ranking between architectures remains unchanged. This is consistent with the theoretical expectation: as the total attention matrix size is fixed at $4 \times d^2$, splitting it into more heads simply redistributes capacity without adding parameters.je ne

### Training time

```{python}
#| label: fig-train-time
#| fig-cap: "Validation loss vs. training time"

fig = go.Figure()
for arch, group in df.groupby("architecture"):
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=group["train_time_s"], y=group["min_val_loss"],
        mode="markers", name=arch, legendgroup=arch,
        marker=dict(color=COLORS.get(arch, "grey"), size=6, opacity=0.5),
        hovertemplate="time=%{x:.0f}s<br>val_loss=%{y:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
    ))
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Training time (s)", yaxis_title="Validation loss",
    legend_title="Architecture", height=430,
)
fig.show()
```

### Interaction: embedding dimension × vocabulary size

```{python}
#| label: fig-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Mean validation loss by embedding dimension and vocabulary size"

from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

arch_order = ["FastText", "Transformer (L=1)", "Transformer (L=2)", "Label Attention"]
arch_order = [a for a in arch_order if a in df["architecture"].unique()]
emb_vals   = sorted(df["emb_dim"].unique())
vocab_vals  = sorted(df["vocab_size"].unique())

vmin = df.groupby(["architecture", "emb_dim", "vocab_size"])["min_val_loss"].mean().min()
vmax = df.groupby(["architecture", "emb_dim", "vocab_size"])["min_val_loss"].mean().max()

fig = make_subplots(rows=2, cols=2, subplot_titles=arch_order)

for idx, arch in enumerate(arch_order):
    row, col = divmod(idx, 2)
    pivot = (
        df[df["architecture"] == arch]
        .groupby(["emb_dim", "vocab_size"])["min_val_loss"].mean()
        .unstack("vocab_size")
        .reindex(index=emb_vals, columns=vocab_vals)
    )
    fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
        z=pivot.values,
        x=[f"{v:,}" for v in vocab_vals],
        y=[str(v) for v in emb_vals],
        colorscale="Viridis_r",
        zmin=vmin, zmax=vmax,
        text=[[f"{v:.3f}" if not np.isnan(v) else "" for v in row_] for row_ in pivot.values],
        texttemplate="%{text}",
        showscale=(idx == 0),
        colorbar=dict(title="Mean val loss", len=0.45, y=0.78) if idx == 0 else None,
        hovertemplate="vocab=%{x}<br>emb_dim=%{y}<br>val_loss=%{z:.4f}<extra>" + arch + "</extra>",
    ), row=row + 1, col=col + 1)
    fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Vocabulary size", row=row + 1, col=col + 1)
    fig.update_yaxes(title_text="Embedding dim", row=row + 1, col=col + 1)

fig.update_layout(height=600)
fig.show()
```

### Prediction analysis

```{python}
#| label: load-predictions-amazon

import numpy as np, tempfile, glob as glob_mod

STAR_LABELS  = ["1★", "2★", "3★", "4★", "5★"]
ARCH_SLUGS   = {
    "FastText":           "fasttext",
    "Transformer (L=1)": "transL1",
    "Transformer (L=2)": "transL2",
    "Label Attention":   "labatt",
}

preds_by_arch = {}   # arch -> {"y_test": ..., "y_pred": ..., "X_test": ...}

for arch, slug in ARCH_SLUGS.items():
    if arch not in df["architecture"].values:
        continue
    cache = Path(f"benchmark/results/predictions_amazon_{slug}.npz")
    if cache.exists():
        d = np.load(cache, allow_pickle=True)
        preds_by_arch[arch] = {
            "y_test": d["y_test"].astype(int),
            "y_pred": d["y_pred"].astype(int),
            "X_test": d["X_test"],
        }
    else:
        run_id = df[df["architecture"] == arch].loc[
            df[df["architecture"] == arch]["min_val_loss"].idxmin(), "run_id"
        ]
        with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp:
            local_dir = mlflow.MlflowClient().download_artifacts(run_id, "predictions", dst_path=tmp)
            npz_file  = glob_mod.glob(f"{local_dir}/*.npz")[0]
            d         = np.load(npz_file, allow_pickle=True)
            preds_by_arch[arch] = {
                "y_test": d["y_test"].astype(int),
                "y_pred": d["y_pred"].astype(int),
                "X_test": d["X_test"],
            }
        np.savez(cache, **preds_by_arch[arch])

ARCHS_AVAIL = list(preds_by_arch.keys())
```

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Confusion matrix

```{python}
#| label: fig-confusion-amazon
#| fig-cap: "Confusion matrix on the Amazon test set"

from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix

n_archs = len(ARCHS_AVAIL)
fig = go.Figure()
for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL):
    d  = preds_by_arch[arch]
    cm = confusion_matrix(d["y_test"], d["y_pred"], normalize="true")
    fig.add_trace(go.Heatmap(
        z=cm,
        x=STAR_LABELS, y=STAR_LABELS,
        colorscale="Blues", zmin=0, zmax=1,
        text=[[f"{v:.2f}" for v in row_] for row_ in cm],
        texttemplate="%{text}",
        colorbar=dict(title="Fraction"),
        hovertemplate="Pred=%{x}<br>True=%{y}<br>fraction=%{z:.3f}<extra></extra>",
        visible=(i == 0),
    ))

fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Predicted", yaxis_title="True",
    yaxis=dict(autorange="reversed"),
    height=500, width=540,
    margin=dict(t=125, r=20),
    updatemenus=[dict(
        type="buttons",
        direction="right",
        buttons=[
            dict(label=arch, method="update",
                 args=[{"visible": [j == i for j in range(n_archs)]}])
            for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL)
        ],
        x=0, xanchor="left",
        y=1.18, yanchor="top",
        showactive=True,
        bgcolor="#f0f0f0",
        bordercolor="#cccccc",
        borderwidth=1,
        font=dict(size=12, color="#222"),
        pad=dict(l=4, r=4, t=4, b=4),
    )],
    annotations=[dict(
        text="<b>Choose the model:</b>", x=0, xanchor="left", xref="paper",
        y=1.21, yanchor="bottom", yref="paper",
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=12, color="#555"),
    )],
)
fig.show()
```

### Error analysis

```{python}
#| label: fig-error-analysis-amazon
#| fig-cap: "Error analysis: prediction distance and directional bias"

import random
from collections import Counter
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
random.seed(42)

SEG_KEYS   = ["Correct", "Off by 1", "Off by 2", "Off by 3+"]
SEG_COLORS = {"Correct": "#43A047", "Off by 1": "#FDD835", "Off by 2": "#FB8C00", "Off by 3+": "#E53935"}
n_archs    = len(ARCHS_AVAIL)
labels_d   = [f"±{dd} star{'s' if dd>1 else ''}" for dd in range(1, 5)]

# Pre-compute per architecture
arch_segs, arch_bias = {}, {}
for arch in ARCHS_AVAIL:
    d      = preds_by_arch[arch]
    y_t, y_p = d["y_test"], d["y_pred"]
    dist   = np.abs(y_p - y_t)
    n      = len(dist)
    arch_segs[arch] = {
        "Correct":   (dist == 0).sum() / n * 100,
        "Off by 1":  (dist == 1).sum() / n * 100,
        "Off by 2":  (dist == 2).sum() / n * 100,
        "Off by 3+": (dist >= 3).sum() / n * 100,
    }
    signed = y_p - y_t
    n_tot  = len(signed)
    arch_bias[arch] = {
        "over":  [(signed ==  dd).sum() / n_tot * 100 for dd in range(1, 5)],
        "under": [(signed == -dd).sum() / n_tot * 100 for dd in range(1, 5)],
    }

fig = make_subplots(
    rows=2, cols=1,
    specs=[[{"type": "xy"}], [{"type": "xy"}]],
    row_heights=[0.28, 0.72],
    vertical_spacing=0.12,
)

# Row 1 — stacked bar (4 traces per arch)
for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL):
    segs = arch_segs[arch]
    for seg in SEG_KEYS:
        pct = segs[seg]
        fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
            x=[pct], y=[""], orientation="h",
            name=seg, legendgroup=seg, showlegend=(i == 0),
            marker_color=SEG_COLORS[seg],
            text=f"<b>{seg}</b><br>{pct:.1f}%",
            textposition="inside", insidetextanchor="middle",
            textfont=dict(size=12, color="white" if seg != "Off by 1" else "#333"),
            hovertemplate=f"<b>{seg}</b>: {pct:.1f}%<extra></extra>",
            visible=(i == 0),
        ), row=1, col=1)

# Row 2 — diverging bar (2 traces per arch)
for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL):
    b = arch_bias[arch]
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=[-u for u in b["under"]], y=labels_d,
        orientation="h", name="Under-predicted",
        legendgroup="under", showlegend=(i == 0),
        marker_color="#1565C0",
        hovertemplate="%{y} — under: <b>%{customdata:.1f}%</b><extra></extra>",
        customdata=b["under"],
        visible=(i == 0),
    ), row=2, col=1)
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
        x=b["over"], y=labels_d,
        orientation="h", name="Over-predicted",
        legendgroup="over", showlegend=(i == 0),
        marker_color="#E53935",
        hovertemplate="%{y} — over: <b>%{x:.1f}%</b><extra></extra>",
        visible=(i == 0),
    ), row=2, col=1)

n_seg_traces  = n_archs * 4
n_bias_traces = n_archs * 2

def make_vis(k):
    v = [False] * (n_seg_traces + n_bias_traces)
    for j in range(4):
        v[k * 4 + j] = True
    v[n_seg_traces + k * 2]     = True
    v[n_seg_traces + k * 2 + 1] = True
    return v

fig.update_xaxes(range=[0, 100], ticksuffix="%", showgrid=False, row=1, col=1)
fig.update_yaxes(showticklabels=False, row=1, col=1)
fig.update_xaxes(
    tickvals=[-15, -10, -5, 0, 5, 10, 15],
    ticktext=["15%", "10%", "5%", "0", "5%", "10%", "15%"],
    title_text="% of test examples", row=2, col=1,
)
fig.update_yaxes(autorange="reversed", row=2, col=1)
fig.add_vline(x=0, line_width=1.5, line_color="#333", row=2, col=1)

fig.update_layout(
    barmode="relative",
    template="simple_white",
    legend=dict(orientation="h", x=0.5, y=-0.20, xanchor="center"),
    height=560, margin=dict(t=110, b=65),
    annotations=[dict(
        text="<b>Choose the model:</b>", x=0, xanchor="left", xref="paper",
        y=1.21, yanchor="bottom", yref="paper",
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=12, color="#555"),
    )],
    updatemenus=[dict(
        type="buttons", direction="right",
        buttons=[
            dict(label=arch, method="update", args=[{"visible": make_vis(i)}])
            for i, arch in enumerate(ARCHS_AVAIL)
        ],
        x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.18, yanchor="top",
        showactive=True, bgcolor="#f0f0f0",
        bordercolor="#cccccc", borderwidth=1,
        font=dict(size=12, color="#222"),
        pad=dict(l=4, r=4, t=4, b=4),
    )],
)
fig.show()
```

### Examples

```{python}
#| label: fig-examples-amazon
#| fig-cap: "Example predictions on the Amazon test set"

import json, random
from IPython.display import HTML

random.seed(42)

y_test = preds_by_arch[ARCHS_AVAIL[0]]["y_test"]
X_test = preds_by_arch[ARCHS_AVAIL[0]]["X_test"]
texts  = X_test.tolist() if X_test.ndim == 1 else X_test[:, 0].tolist()

df_ex = pd.DataFrame({"true": y_test, "text": texts})
for arch in ARCHS_AVAIL:
    df_ex[arch] = preds_by_arch[arch]["y_pred"]

df_ex["disagree"] = df_ex[ARCHS_AVAIL].nunique(axis=1) > 1
df_ex["any_wrong"] = (df_ex[ARCHS_AVAIL].values != df_ex[["true"]].values).any(axis=1)
pool   = df_ex[df_ex["disagree"] & df_ex["any_wrong"]]
sample = pool.sample(12, random_state=42).reset_index(drop=True)

ARCH_SHORT = {"FastText": "FastText", "Transformer (L=1)": "Trans (L=1)",
              "Transformer (L=2)": "Trans (L=2)", "Label Attention": "Label Att"}

examples = [
    {"true": int(r["true"]), "text": str(r["text"]),
     "preds": {a: int(r[a]) for a in ARCHS_AVAIL}}
    for _, r in sample.iterrows()
]

html = f"""
<style>
  .ex-wrap  {{ font-family:sans-serif; font-size:14px; max-width:100%; }}
  .ex-card  {{ border:1px solid #ddd; border-radius:8px; padding:20px 24px;
               background:#fafafa; margin-bottom:14px; }}
  .ex-meta  {{ font-size:13px; color:#555; margin-bottom:10px; }}
  .ex-meta b {{ color:#222; }}
  .ex-text  {{ line-height:1.75; color:#222; margin-bottom:18px; }}
  .ex-preds {{ display:flex; gap:12px; flex-wrap:wrap; }}
  .ex-model {{ display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; gap:4px; }}
  .ex-mname {{ font-size:11px; color:#888; font-weight:600; text-transform:uppercase;
               letter-spacing:.04em; }}
  .ex-badge {{ padding:5px 14px; border-radius:5px; font-weight:700; font-size:15px; }}
  .correct  {{ background:#d4edda; color:#155724; }}
  .wrong    {{ background:#f8d7da; color:#721c24; }}
  .ex-nav   {{ display:flex; align-items:center; gap:12px; }}
  .ex-btn   {{ padding:6px 18px; background:#f0f0f0; border:1px solid #ccc;
               border-radius:5px; cursor:pointer; font-size:13px; }}
  .ex-btn:hover {{ background:#e0e0e0; }}
  .ex-count {{ font-size:12px; color:#999; }}
</style>

<div class="ex-wrap">
  <div class="ex-nav" style="justify-content:center; margin-bottom:12px;">
    <button class="ex-btn" onclick="exNav(-1)">← Prev</button>
    <span class="ex-count" id="ex-count"></span>
    <button class="ex-btn" onclick="exNav(1)">Next →</button>
  </div>
  <div class="ex-card">
    <div class="ex-meta" id="ex-meta"></div>
    <div class="ex-text" id="ex-text"></div>
    <div class="ex-preds" id="ex-preds"></div>
  </div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {{
  const EX   = {json.dumps(examples)};
  const SL   = {json.dumps(STAR_LABELS)};
  const AS   = {json.dumps(ARCH_SHORT)};
  let cur = 0;

  function render(i) {{
    const e = EX[i];
    document.getElementById('ex-meta').innerHTML =
      'True rating: <b>' + SL[e.true] + '</b>';
    document.getElementById('ex-text').textContent = e.text;
    let h = '';
    for (const [arch, pred] of Object.entries(e.preds)) {{
      const cls = pred === e.true ? 'correct' : 'wrong';
      h += '<div class="ex-model"><span class="ex-mname">' + (AS[arch]||arch) + '</span>'
         + '<span class="ex-badge ' + cls + '">' + SL[pred] + '</span></div>';
    }}
    document.getElementById('ex-preds').innerHTML = h;
    document.getElementById('ex-count').textContent = (i+1) + ' / ' + EX.length;
  }}

  window.exNav = function(d) {{ cur = (cur + d + EX.length) % EX.length; render(cur); }};
  render(0);
}})();
</script>
"""
HTML(html)
```

:::

## IMDb

*Coming soon.*

:::
